item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is totally my jam. While I do think that the ending was a tiny bit underwhelming, I do really appreciate the detail given to each of these pieces, particularly with how you combined both Renaissance and SCP universe (particularly the Hanged King) imagery into all these cohesive pieces. Really made for an intriguing read. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm such a sucker for the very specific genre that is writing that includes descriptions of fictional artwork inspired by Renaissance religious imagery, so, yeah, this completely tickled my fancy. Very nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Rodin's Gates of Hell is definitely way too famous to be used (and also, not Renaissance) - but an interesting odd collection regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, didn’t notice the canvas part. Pardons

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The painting was removed from the Papal Palace multiple times after the death of Julius III in 1555, but always reappeared in the same place the following day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really liked this.I’m just wondering what part of this requires the “teleportation” tag

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this whole article, but Trinity of the Red Court is just so killer I can't handle it. The Hanged King allusion, the excellent escalation of tension, it's the perfect capstone to a good idea.

A jester is added to the lower left corner of the painting, pointing at the deceased Christ.
Young children appear behind the jester in a long line that extends into the darkness.

This has to be Bobble, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I approve of this message, the idea of a museum full of anomalous paintings is really neat. Would definitley read about/see all other instances. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly I think The Gates of Hell is too famous to work as an image in this article, and it drags me right out of the narrative seeing it passed off as 15th-century; especially when -089 is probably the weakest of the entries and seems to exist in service of the image.

Otherwise this is a strong piece full of intriguing mini-articles, while still managing to build to an appropriate crescendo (and not just because I'm a sucker for Hanged King Mythos articles).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe not what you were going for at all, but I get super strong All Along the Watchtower vibes from that last entry.

Very creative piece; I don't think anart has ever grabbed me as effectively as it does here, given the historical context and the anthology-style description. +1

Edit: I'm also hopeful that, given so many other pieces in this exhibit, maybe we'll see an expanded list in the future?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. It's a neat collection of anomalous art, and I have to wonder what the other 200+ works are like. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely love this; I would happily read the entire archive catalog if it existed. It just all feels so nice and grounded, for lack of a better word. And it's nice to see aniart as something that stretches beyond the post-modern reality fuckery of AWCY.

Would upvote again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really spectacular; I rarely note anything 'crazy' in the tags but these caused me to go back and reread everything immediately. +1 from a refugee of an art history department.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This feels like a disturbing interpretation of moving/living/ghost-of-someone artwork from the Harry Potter universe, and I dig it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you very much! I'm surprised I missed that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, this year's Met Gala got weird…
(Joking aside, I absolutely adore this!! +1)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was brilliant and thoroughly enjoyable.

Minor pedant point: Cardinal del Monte was the Pope's adopted nephew, not son.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Beautiful stuff! Telling so many satisfying micro stories is a difficult feat, as is mimicking the stylistic elements of other artwork, but you succeeded at both with flying colors.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An excellent work, enjoyed a lot its reading.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ayyy, I remember reviewing this! Always nice to see a new angle on anart, especially one that leans away from relying on the anartist themselves describing their intentions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want a full interpretation of SCP-4505-156 on my desk by tommorow. Please take into account the artists biography and beliefs, the time of creation, as well as its anomalous properties.
This work will make 15% of your final grade.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The stone, it called to me
And now I see the things the stone has shown to me
A rock that spoke a word
An animated mineral, it can be heard

This started out as an attempt to collaborate with Djoric on a Horizon Initiative skip five or six years ago. I invited several other authors to contribute over the years, but nobody else really took to the idea the way I did. After years of tinkering, I felt like it was high time I pulled this out of storage and put it on display where it belongs.

Images sources: 1 2

Discover more of my weird ramblings in
~PeppersGhost's Gallery of Illusion!~
